story:
Ma turns sirens and shaking skies into a game. “When the sky makes loud noises, run as fast as you can!” she says, and I pretend I’m a rabbit outracing foxes. She even made a Thunder Tune—run, run, run, fast, fast, fast—so I’ll sprint to the shelter before the roar catches me. Sometimes her hands tremble when we sing. I ask why; she fixes my hair. “Nothing for you to worry about, sunshine.” If I run fast, she says there might be a chocolate bar later. I don’t think she likes the game, but I play anyway.

I’m Zara. I’m six and a half, almost seven. I love pink, Barbies, and puppies; I hate broccoli, math, and the thunder that never stops. When I grow up, I’ll be a unicorn ballerina like Belle on my favorite show. Ma says ballet helps me run, so I practice my quick feet. I used to go to the studio before it closed. One day I’ll dance for real, glowing unicorn and all.

Today, I’m running for real. Not to show off, but because I lost Ma.

Leila—my BFF, already eight, practically a grown-up—said we should get gummy bears. We went to Mr. Nazim’s shop, but he had none, again. We stepped outside, and boom. The sharp buzzing I know from whispers and warnings sliced the air. I told Leila we had to be quick, quick, quick, like mice. People rushed to their doors; shutters slammed. Ma says we run to the secret lair. That’s where a princess is waiting, and we’re warrior princesses who must save her before the villains do.

We dashed. The street flashed. A high beep, a white burst, and then the world went quiet, then screaming. We woke on the ground. Grey snow sifted down—like crystals, I thought—until the bitter taste told me it wasn’t snow. Dust, ash, the sky falling in pieces. Mr. Nazim’s shop was buried in grey. We had to help him, I said, but Leila only whimpered. Rubble pinned her stomach. Red soaked the white of her shirt. I called it paint because I wanted it to be paint.
Before she moved her mouth, I knew she was going to tell me, “You have to go. Find your mom and the shelter
“Zara,” she whispered, brushing dust from my eyebrows, “I’ve fallen. You have to go. Find your mom and the shelter.” I saluted like a soldier. She saluted back with one hand; the other pressed the red. I promised to return after I saved the princess.

Voices boomed behind me—three tall men shouting to surrender. In my head, they were the villains. I ran. I tripped, scraped my knee, and cried because ballerinas don’t fall like that. I wanted Ma. I pushed up and limped toward our garden and the hole in the ground.

I won the game. I dropped into the bunker before the second thunder. Ma grabbed me and wouldn’t let go. Grandmama said it was a miracle, then whispered about drone strikes. Leila’s parents stood behind us; when Ma shook her head, they folded. Ma explained the red wasn’t paint. This was one hurt she couldn’t kiss better. I turned cold and small.

Somewhere, a radio hummed: military aggression, bombings of the capital. The second thunder hit. Ma covered my ears and told me not to look, but I peeked through the entrance anyway. The sky shattered into not-fireworks, smoke and fire spitting like dragons. The grey snow darkened and thickened, and then it wasn’t grey anymore.

I’m hungry. There are no gummy bears, and Ma has no chocolate bar after all. I don’t want to play the game anymore.
